Member-only story

A comparison of six AI agent spec formats organized into two families, evaluated by precedence and scope rules, with a decision matrix for what to adopt when.

12 min read
Just now

--

Everyone commits an instruction file for their AI agent and treats it like configuration, but it’s just text glued onto the prompt — and the model can ignore it. One documented case shows exactly where that leads: a real CLAUDE.md bloated so badly it was eating over 42,000 tokens on every conversation turn. An independent ETH Zurich study found something even less comfortable: auto-generated context files can raise costs by more than 20% without improving effectiveness — while manually written, minimal files actually do better. This affects anyone who runs an agent against a production repo every day.
The market presents these formats as contestants in one ranking: DESIGN.md versus AGENTS.md versus Spec Kit versus CLAUDE.md. That’s the wrong question. Over the next ten minutes, you’ll see that these six formats aren’t six variants of one thing — they’re two families plus DESIGN.md — and you’ll walk away with a matrix for what to adopt in your own repo, not another neutral comparison table.
The One Distinction That Matters Before…
